section.title: The Altar on Mount Ebal
section.sectionRationale: In the middle of the conquest, Israel pauses to worship and to hear God's word. At Mount Ebal, exactly as Moses had commanded, Joshua builds an altar of uncut stones, offers sacrifices, writes out a copy of the law, and reads aloud all its blessings and curses before the whole assembly. It is one brief but weighty movement, the covenant renewal that grounds the conquest in worship and obedience, reminding Israel that the land is held only under the word of God.
section.thesis: Israel does not merely fight for the land; it receives the land as a covenant people, so in obedience to Moses' command they stop to worship at an altar, to write and hear the whole law with its blessings and curses, and to place themselves afresh under the God whose word alone secures their future in the land.
section.frameNote: Two beats: the altar and sacrifices on Mount Ebal with the law inscribed on stones, verses 30 to 32; and the whole assembly gathered between the mountains to hear all the blessings and curses of the law, verses 33 to 35.

units.label: The altar and the law on stones

verses.text: At that time Joshua built an {{mizbeach|altar}} on Mount Ebal to the LORD, the God of Israel,

just as Moses the servant of the LORD had commanded the Israelites. He built it according to what is written in the Book of the Law of Moses: “an altar of uncut stones on which no iron tool has been used.” And on it they offered burnt offerings to the LORD, and they sacrificed peace offerings.
And there in the presence of the Israelites, Joshua inscribed on the stones a copy of the law of Moses, which he had written.

units.label: The whole assembly hears the blessings and curses
units.note: not a word that Joshua failed to read

verses.text: All Israel, foreigners and citizens alike, with their elders, officers, and judges, stood on both sides of the ark of the covenant of the LORD facing the Levitical priests who carried it. Half of the people stood in front of Mount Gerizim and half of them in front of Mount Ebal, as Moses the servant of the LORD had commanded earlier, to bless the people of Israel.
Afterward, Joshua read aloud all the words of the {{torah|law}}, the {{berakhah|blessings}} and the curses, according to all that is written in the Book of the Law.
There was not a word of all that Moses had commanded that Joshua failed to read before the whole assembly of Israel, including the women, the little ones, and the foreigners who lived among them.

mizbeach.gloss: Altar, the place of sacrifice, literally a place of slaughtering. Built of uncut stones untouched by iron tools, the altar keeps the worship humble and God-defined, unshaped by human artistry or violence. Here Israel offers burnt offerings and peace offerings, grounding the conquest in atonement and fellowship with God rather than in military self-confidence.

torah.gloss: Law, instruction, teaching. Joshua writes a copy of the torah on stones and reads all of it aloud, exactly as Moses commanded. The land is entered and held under the torah; Israel's whole life in Canaan is to be shaped by God's revealed word, which is why the covenant is renewed by hearing every word of it.

berakhah.gloss: Blessing, the good God pronounces and gives to those who keep His covenant. Read out from Mount Gerizim alongside the curses from Mount Ebal, the berakhah sets before Israel the two ways: life and good for obedience, judgment for turning away. The covenant renewal makes plain that the land is held under blessing conditioned on faithfulness to the LORD.

basic.context: Right in the middle of the conquest, between the fall of Ai and the campaigns ahead, Israel stops to do something that has nothing to do with fighting: they worship and renew the covenant. Following instructions Moses had given long before, Joshua builds a plain altar of uncut stones on Mount Ebal, and they offer sacrifices. He writes out a copy of God's law on stones, and then, with the whole nation gathered between Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim, he reads aloud the entire law, both its blessings and its curses. Everyone is there, men, women, children, and the foreigners among them. The question underneath is: what really holds Israel's future in the land, their swords or their God's word?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative recording an act of covenant renewal commanded in Deuteronomy 27. It shows Israel deliberately grounding the conquest in worship and obedience. The specific ceremony belongs to the old covenant, but its enduring lesson is clear: God's people live under His word, and their standing is a covenant matter of blessing and curse, faithfulness and warning. The reading of the whole law to everyone underlines that all God's people are to know His word.
basic.originalLanguages: Joshua builds a plain mizbeach, an altar of uncut stones, for humble, God-defined worship. He writes and reads the whole torah, God's law, under which the land is held. And he reads aloud the berakhah, the blessing, alongside the curses, setting the two ways before the nation.
basic.typology: The altar with its burnt and peace offerings points to Christ, our sacrifice and our peace with God. The blessings and curses of the law, set before Israel, point to Christ, who bore the curse of the law so His people might receive the blessing promised to Abraham. The law written and read anticipates the word written on the heart in the new covenant.
basic.christ: The sacrifices on Ebal point to Jesus, the true burnt offering and the peace offering who reconciles us to God. The two mountains of blessing and curse frame the whole gospel: Christ took the curse of Ebal on the cross so that the blessing of Gerizim might come to all who trust Him. And the law read to all foreshadows the word of Christ dwelling richly in His people.
basic.god: God is the covenant Lord who binds His people to Himself by His word. He defines how He will be worshiped, receiving humble offerings on an unhewn altar. He sets before His people both blessing and curse, taking their obedience seriously. And He includes everyone, citizen and foreigner, in the hearing of His word, showing that all His people are to live under it.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 27 commands this very ceremony at Ebal and Gerizim. Exodus 20 requires an altar of uncut stones. Galatians 3 shows Christ bearing the curse to bring the blessing. And Deuteronomy 31 commands the public reading of the law to all Israel.
basic.oneStory: In the thick of conquest, Israel stops to worship and to place itself under God's word, remembering that the land is held only by covenant faithfulness. The altar and the blessings and curses point forward to Christ, who bore the curse of the law so that His people might inherit the blessing and live under His word written on the heart.

context.literary: This short passage interrupts the conquest narrative with a scene of worship, deliberately slowing the action to make a theological point: the campaign is framed by covenant, not just combat. It fulfills to the letter the commands of Deuteronomy 27, and its repeated just as Moses had commanded stresses careful obedience. The setting between Mount Ebal, the mount of curses, and Mount Gerizim, the mount of blessings, dramatizes the two ways set before Israel. The reading of every word to the whole assembly, women, children, and foreigners included, closes the scene on the completeness of Israel's submission to the word.
context.historical: Mount Ebal and Mount Gerizim stand over Shechem in the central highlands, forming a natural amphitheater where a large assembly could hear a voice carry. Shechem was where Abraham first built an altar in the land, Genesis 12:6-7, giving the site deep covenant memory. An altar of uncut stones, untouched by iron, was commanded to keep worship free of human ornamentation or the tools of war and violence. Writing law on plastered or inscribed stones was a known way to publish a covenant text. That Israel could march into the central hill country to hold this ceremony shows how far the conquest had already broken Canaanite resistance.
context.canonical: The ceremony fulfills Deuteronomy 27 and 11, binding Joshua's conquest tightly to the law of Moses. The theme of blessing and curse set before God's people runs through the whole covenant and reaches its resolution in the New Testament, where Christ bears the curse to secure the blessing, Galatians 3:13-14. The public reading of all the law to everyone anticipates later covenant renewals, such as Josiah's and Ezra's, and the New Testament's call for the word of Christ to dwell richly in all God's people.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show Israel, in obedience to Moses, grounding the conquest in worship and covenant renewal, building the commanded altar, offering sacrifices, and hearing the whole law with its blessings and curses, so that the people would know they hold the land only under the word of God.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific ceremony at Ebal and Gerizim belongs to the old covenant and is not a rite the church repeats. What endures is the pattern: God's people are to worship as He directs, to live under His word, to take seriously the difference between covenant faithfulness and unfaithfulness, and to ensure that all, including children and outsiders, know God's word.

debates.question: Why does the narrative pause the conquest for a worship ceremony at this point?

views.label: To ground the whole conquest in covenant obedience
views.case: By stopping to fulfill Moses' command exactly, Israel declares that the land is not simply seized by force but received under covenant; the careful obedience, the humble altar, and the reading of the whole law frame the entire campaign as a matter of faithfulness to God's word, not military ambition.

views.label: To renew commitment after failure and before further conquest
views.case: Coming right after the sin of Achan and the defeat at Ai, the ceremony re-anchors a chastened people in the blessings and curses of the covenant before they go on, reminding them that continued success depends on obedience, so the pause is a spiritual reset at a crucial hinge in the story.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that the passage subordinates the conquest to the covenant: Israel's future in the land rests on hearing and heeding God's word, a truth as vital for the church as for Israel.

study.originalLanguages: Three words carry the weight of this covenant pause. First, Joshua builds a mizbeach, an altar, of uncut stones on which no iron tool has been used, keeping the worship humble and entirely God-defined, shaped neither by human artistry nor by the implements of war; on it Israel offers burnt offerings, which speak of atonement, and peace offerings, which speak of fellowship, so that the conquest is rooted in reconciliation with God rather than in military pride. Second, Joshua writes and then reads aloud the whole torah, the law, the instruction of God; the land is entered and held under the torah, and Israel's entire life in Canaan is to be governed by God's revealed word, which is why not one word of it is left unread. Third, he reads the berakhah, the blessing, from the direction of Gerizim alongside the curses from Ebal, setting before the nation the two ways: life and good for covenant faithfulness, judgment for turning away. Altar, law, blessing: the vocabulary makes the scene a deliberate act of placing the whole people back under the God of the covenant.

typology.type: The altar with its burnt and peace offerings
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true burnt offering who atones and the peace offering who reconciles us to God, Ephesians 5:2 and Colossians 1:20.

typology.type: The blessings and curses of the law set before Israel
typology.fulfillment: Christ bearing the curse of the law so the blessing of Abraham comes to all who believe, Galatians 3:13-14.

typology.type: The whole law written and read to all the people
typology.fulfillment: The new covenant word written on the heart and the word of Christ dwelling richly in His people, Jeremiah 31:33 and Colossians 3:16.

study.christ: This scene frames the gospel between two mountains. On Mount Ebal the curses of the law are read, the judgment that falls on all who fail to keep God's covenant; on Mount Gerizim the blessings are proclaimed. Every Israelite stood exposed to both, for none could keep the whole law. The New Testament tells us how the two are resolved: Christ redeemed us from the curse of the law by becoming a curse for us, so that in Him the blessing promised to Abraham might come to the nations, Galatians 3:13-14. He is also the altar's true fulfillment, the burnt offering who atones and the peace offering who is Himself our peace, having reconciled us to God through His blood, Ephesians 2:14 and Colossians 1:20. And the law written and read to all foreshadows the new covenant, in which God writes His word on His people's hearts and the word of Christ dwells richly among them. Israel's covenant pause at Ebal points forward to the One who bore the curse and secured the blessing.
study.god: The God revealed here is the covenant Lord who claims His people through His word. He does not leave worship to human invention but defines it, receiving humble offerings on an altar of unhewn stone; He will be approached on His terms, through atonement and fellowship. He takes His people's obedience with full seriousness, setting before them both blessing and curse so that they cannot presume upon the land as if it were theirs unconditionally; it is held under covenant. And He is a God whose word is for everyone: men, women, little ones, and the foreigners living among them all stand and hear, for all His people are to live under His revealed will. This is a God who binds Himself to a people and binds that people to Himself by His word, and who will finally, in Christ, take the curse upon Himself to keep them in His blessing.

crossReferences.note: Moses commands the altar of uncut stones on Mount Ebal and the writing of the law on stones, obeyed exactly here.
The blessing on Gerizim and the curse on Ebal, the arrangement Israel now enacts.
An altar of uncut stones, untouched by iron tools, the pattern Joshua follows.
Christ became a curse for us to redeem us, so the blessing of Abraham might come to the nations.
Let the word of Christ dwell in you richly, the new covenant echo of the law read to all Israel.

study.oneStory: In the thick of the conquest, Israel deliberately stops to worship and renew the covenant, building the commanded altar, offering sacrifices, and hearing the whole law with its blessings and curses, so that everyone knows the land is held only under God's word. The scene points forward to Christ, who is our true altar and peace offering, who bore the curse of Ebal so that the blessing of Gerizim might come to all who trust Him, and who writes His word on the hearts of His people in the new covenant.

quiz.q: What did Joshua build on Mount Ebal, and how?

options.text: An altar of uncut stones, untouched by iron tools, just as Moses had commanded.
options.note: Verses 30 to 31 describe the humble, God-defined altar built in obedience to the law.

options.text: A grand carved altar decorated with gold.
options.note: It was deliberately of uncut stones, plain and undecorated, not a grand carved altar.

options.text: A fortress to guard the central hill country.
options.note: He built an altar for worship, not a fortress; the pause was for covenant, not defense.

options.text: A monument boasting of Israel's victories.
options.note: It was an altar to the LORD for sacrifice, not a boastful victory monument.

quiz.q: What did Joshua read aloud to the whole assembly?

options.text: All the words of the law, both its blessings and its curses, leaving nothing out.
options.note: Verses 34 to 35 stress that he read every word, the blessings and the curses.

options.text: Only the blessings, to encourage the people after victory.
options.note: He read the blessings and the curses together; he did not read the blessings alone.

options.text: A short summary of the most important commands.
options.note: He read all the words of the law, not a summary; nothing was left out.

options.text: A record of the battles Israel had won.
options.note: He read the law of Moses, not a battle record.

quiz.q: Who was included in the assembly that heard the law?

options.text: All Israel, including women, little ones, and the foreigners living among them.
options.note: Verses 33 and 35 stress the whole assembly, citizens and foreigners alike, heard the word.

options.text: Only the priests and the fighting men.
options.note: The soldiers and leaders were there, but so were women, children, and foreigners; all heard.

options.text: Only the adult male citizens of Israel.
options.note: The text specifically includes women, little ones, and foreigners, not only male citizens.

options.text: Only Joshua and the elders in private.
options.note: The law was read before the whole assembly, not privately to the leaders.

quiz.q: Why did Israel pause the conquest to hold this ceremony?

options.text: To ground the conquest in worship and covenant, remembering the land is held only under God's word.
options.note: The obedient altar and the reading of the whole law frame the campaign as a matter of covenant faithfulness.

options.text: To rest the troops and celebrate their military skill.
options.note: It was worship and covenant renewal, not a celebration of Israel's skill or a mere rest.

options.text: Because the fighting was completely finished.
options.note: Much conquest still lay ahead; the pause was a spiritual reset in the middle of the campaign.

options.text: Because God had commanded them to stop conquering for good.
options.note: God had not ended the conquest; He had commanded this covenant ceremony through Moses.

questions: In the middle of a demanding campaign, Israel stopped to worship and hear God's word. When life is busy and pressured, how do you keep worship and Scripture central rather than crowded out?
The altar was plain and undecorated, worship on God's terms rather than human invention. Are there ways you are tempted to reshape worship around your own preferences rather than God's word?
Every word of the law was read to everyone, including children and outsiders. How seriously do you take knowing God's word yourself and passing it on to those around you?
The blessings and curses set two ways before Israel. How does taking God's word seriously, both its promises and its warnings, shape the way you live?
Christ bore the curse of Ebal so the blessing of Gerizim could come to you. How does knowing Jesus took your curse deepen your gratitude and your desire to live under His word?

translationNotes.note: The altar of uncut stones on which no iron tool has been used quotes the law; the BSB footnote points to Exodus 20:25 and Deuteronomy 27:5.
Mount Gerizim, in front of half the people, was the mount of blessing, and Mount Ebal the mount of curses, as commanded in Deuteronomy 11 and 27.
The BSB sets off the blessings and the curses with em dashes; these have been rendered as commas here, with no change to the wording.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 8:30-35. Prose narrative. The em dashes in verse 34 were converted to commas; no em or en dashes remain anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
